params.text:
Fire extinguishers

Extinguishers are for tackling a very small fire only – and only if it is safe to do so, you have a clear escape route behind you, and you have raised the alarm first. Your safety and the residents' safety always come first: if there is any doubt, get out and let the fire service deal with it. Using the wrong type of extinguisher can make a fire far worse, so it is vital to know what each colour is for.

In the UK, extinguisher bodies are red with a coloured label or band showing the type. Tap the cards below to learn the five main types.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: The five main fire extinguisher types used in the UK.

dialogs.answer: For Class A fires – solid materials such as wood, paper, textiles and furniture. Never use on electrical equipment or burning fat/oil.
For Class A and Class B fires – solids and flammable liquids. A common all-round extinguisher. Not for use on cooking oil fires or live electrics.
Carbon dioxide, for electrical fires and flammable liquids. It smothers the fire by displacing oxygen and leaves no residue, so it is safe on electronics.
A multi-purpose "ABC" extinguisher for solids, liquids and gases. Avoid using in enclosed spaces as the powder can be inhaled and reduces visibility.
For Class F fires – cooking oils and deep-fat fats in kitchens. Cools and seals the burning oil. Not for most other fire types.

params.text:
The PASS technique

If you ever need to use an extinguisher on a small fire, remember PASS:

  • P – Pull the safety pin.
  • A – Aim the nozzle at the base of the fire, not the flames.
  • S – Squeeze the handle to release the extinguishing agent.
  • S – Sweep from side to side across the base of the fire.

Always keep an escape route behind you and back away if the fire does not go out quickly.
